elements.sent-2411-001: Individuals have been increasingly interacting with online Large Language Models (LLMs), both in their work and personal lives.
elements.sent-2411-002: These interactions raise privacy issues as the LLMs are typically hosted by third-parties who can gather a variety of sensitive information about users and their companies.
elements.sent-2411-003: Text Sanitization techniques have been proposed in the literature and can be used to sanitize user prompts before sending them to the LLM.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, sanitization has an impact on the downstream task performed by the LLM, and often to such an extent that it leads to unacceptable results for the user.
elements.sent-2411-005: This is not just a minor annoyance, with clear monetary consequences as LLM services charge on a per use basis as well as great amount of computing resources wasted.
elements.sent-2411-006: We propose an architecture leveraging a Small Language Model (SLM) at the user-side to help estimate the impact of sanitization on a prompt before it is sent to the LLM, thus preventing resource losses.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our evaluation of this architecture revealed a significant problem with text sanitization based on Differential Privacy, on which we want to draw the attention of the community for further investigation.
elements.sent-2411-008: Large Language Models (LLMs) have transformed several domains and are now being used for a variety of tasks such as document retrieval, code explanation, document summarization, and image generation.
elements.sent-2411-009: The largest models, with respect to the number of parameters, yield the strongest performance~\cite{wei2022Emergent .}
elements.sent-2411-010: As a consequence, these large models require significant computing resources for training, which only a few entities worldwide possess the capability to provide.
elements.sent-2411-011: Even after the training phase, querying the largest models remains resource-intensive and beyond the capabilities of an average personal computer.
elements.sent-2411-012: LLMs are then typically hosted by these same entities and made available online as a service (either free or paid) that users query (or prompt) and receive a response\footnote{e.g., \url{https://chatgpt.com/ or \url{https://mistral.ai/ .}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: During this process, any sensitive information contained within the prompt is received by the service provider.
elements.sent-2411-014: Sharing information through LLM prompts raises privacy concerns in both personal and professional settings.
elements.sent-2411-015: For instance, leaked trade secrets can result in job loss \footnote{\href{https://www.techradar.com/news/samsung-workers-leaked-company-secrets-by-using-chatgpt {techradar.com/news/samsung-workers-leaked-company-secrets-by-using-chatgpt .}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: In addition to data being revealed to the provider itself, prompts may also be used to improve LLMs, which in turn are known to memorize their training data and may disclose them when subsequently queried by other users \cite{carlini2019secret .}
elements.sent-2411-017: While prompts can be manually redacted and sanitized by human experts, such process is time consuming and often not practical in several applications.
elements.sent-2411-018: Some of the recent works \cite{chen2023Hide, chong2024Casper, papadopoulou2023Neural leverage Named Entity Recognition machine learning models to find specific, privacy-exposing elements in the text for sanitization purposes.}
elements.sent-2411-019: The sanitization process itself is generally carried out either using Differential Privacy (DP) applied on text \cite{feyisetan2020Privacy, yue2021Differential , Generalization \cite{hassan2023Utilitypreserving, olstadGenerationSelectionReplacement2023 or rewriting assisted by language models \cite{weggenmann2022DPVAE, igamberdiev2023DPBART .}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, altering the text during the sanitization process can reduce its utility\cite{zhang2024No .}
elements.sent-2411-021: If the text is a prompt, the downstream task performed by the LLM (e.g., summarization) might suffer from this utility loss.
elements.sent-2411-022: Excessive utility loss can result in wasted resources for the provider and unnecessary expenses for the user, especially when LLM usage incurs a fee.
elements.sent-2411-023: In this paper, we address the following problem of practical concern.
elements.sent-2411-024: To prevent employees from inadvertently submitting sensitive or Personally Identifiable Information (PII) as prompts, the company aims to sanitize user prompts before they are sent to the LLM provider.
elements.sent-2411-025: However, a too great utility loss caused by the sanitization is undesirable from the company's perspective as it incurs costs for every prompt sent to the LLM provider.
elements.sent-2411-026: To avoid such resource wastage, the company deploys a Small Language Model (SLM) on the client-side (either on the employee’s device or at the company level).
elements.sent-2411-027: The SLM's task is to alert the user if the sanitized text would not yield meaningful results when queried through the online LLM.
elements.sent-2411-028: This approach ensures the company and its employees get the best value for their money by balancing privacy, utility, and financial resources.
elements.sent-2411-029: For this purpose, we investigated the benefit of a small language model running ``locally'' at the end-user level to efficiently assess the utility loss of a given sanitized prompt when processed by an LLM.
elements.sent-2411-030: DP being currently the prevailing framework for privacy-respecting computations, we investigate a sanitization method employing distance-based DP for text \cite{feyisetan2020Privacy, qu2021Natural, yue2021Differential where noise is added to textual data represented as $n$-dimensional vectors.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Our experiments shed light on a particular issue with this sanitization method, which severely hinders its capacity to preserve semantic integrity.
elements.sent-2411-032: Overall, we make the following contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-033: We propose an architecture where a Small Language Model is leveraged at user-side to gauge the quality of a privacy-preserving text sanitization of a given prompt intended to be processed by a LLM for inference.
elements.sent-2411-034: We refer to this process as ``resource-constrained privacy preserving LLM interaction''.
elements.sent-2411-035: We empirically evaluate such sanitization process for a summarization task performed on a publicly available dataset, leveraging open and public SLM and LLM.
elements.sent-2411-036: We show the colossal impact of sanitization where none of the models are able to produce results of any utility for amounts of noise usually considered non-private in classic DP (e.g., $\varepsilon=35)$.
elements.sent-2411-037: Even with less destructive sanitization, the process affects different texts to varying degrees, sometimes in completely opposite ways where an LLM receiving equally sanitized texts can produce summaries having similarities with the original texts ranging from 0.0 to 0.9.
elements.sent-2411-038: This justifies the introduction of a utility assessor to guide the sanitization process.
elements.sent-2411-039: We release the code of the experiments\footnote{\url{https://github.com/inathwor/Preempting-Text-Sanitization .}}
elements.sent-2411-040: We uncover a significant issue with distance-based DP for text which questions its semantic preservation.
elements.sent-2411-041: We faithfully reproduced some experiments from \cite{feyisetan2020Privacy and show that a potential performance-driven implementation choice completely changes the capacity of distance-based DP for text to output closely-related words, as it is expected to do for utility-preserving sanitization.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Instead, using an implementation rigorously following the definition in the same experiment will output the exact same word 99\% of the time on average, not providing any privacy protection.
elements.sent-2411-043: We also release the code of these experiments\footnotemark[3].
elements.sent-2411-044: In essence, this paper intends to pave the way for further research on the problem of reconciling privacy, utility and resource consumption in the context of LLM prompts.
elements.sent-2411-045: It also highlights the need for additional study on distance-based DP applied on text, where the privacy or utility guarantees are heavily dependent on implementation choices.
elements.sent-2411-046: Ideally, a reference implementation will facilitate widespread correct usage.
elements.sent-2411-047: In this section we provide necessary background information about differential privacy, how concepts of differential privacy can be applied in the text domain, and the specific technique for text sanitization used in our work.
elements.sent-2411-048: Differential privacy (DP) is a formal framework to provide privacy in data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-049: Informally, an algorithm handling a set of data is said to be differentially private if its output is not significantly affected by the presence or absence of one particular input~\cite{dwork2014algorithmic .}
elements.sent-2411-050: DP is achieved by adding properly calibrated noise to the result of the computation itself or to the input data in the case of Local DP \cite{kasiviswanathanWhatCanWe2011 .}
elements.sent-2411-051: While DP is usually applied on structured data (numbers, tabular etc.), applications on unstructured data have also been proposed \cite{zhao2022survey in particular on text \cite{feyisetan2020Privacy,yue2021Differential,qu2021Natural .}}
elements.sent-2411-052: We primarily focus on this particular sanitization method because differential privacy is currently regarded as the gold standard for privacy-preserving data publication \cite{balle2020hypothesis , without reliance on the performance of a paired PII detector as it sanitizes a text in its entirety.}
elements.sent-2411-053: We refer the reader to \cref{sec:sota for an overview of the state of the art in general text sanitization techniques.}
elements.sent-2411-054: For this work, we leverage distance-based DP, denoted $\dx$-privacy, a generalization of Local DP where the domain of input data is evaluated by an arbitrary distance metric $d$ \cite{chatzikokolakis2013Broadening , whereby Local DP explicitly defines Hamming distance \cite{kasiviswanathanWhatCanWe2011 .}}
elements.sent-2411-055: An example of $\dx$-privacy is proposed in \cite{andres2013Geoindistinguishability , called Geo-indistinguishability, where the authors avoid disclosure of a user's exact geographic location by releasing a differentially private substitute.}
elements.sent-2411-056: In this example, the metric used is the Euclidean Distance with closer locations favored over more distant ones from the original.
elements.sent-2411-057: The radius of what is considered close depends on the privacy parameter $\varepsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-058: Applying $\dx$-privacy on text is made possible with the development of vector representations of text, including GloVe \cite{pennington2014GloVe , fastText \cite{bojanowski2017Enriching or the inner vocabulary of language models \cite{devlin2019BERT .}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: These vocabularies are trained on vast quantities of text to progressively learn the representation of tokens (i.e., a word or a sub-word) as $n$-dimensional vectors called embeddings where the distances in the embedding space express the semantic difference between tokens.
elements.sent-2411-060: Sanitization of a text is then performed by adding differentially private noise to the embeddings of tokens in the text \cite{feyisetan2020Privacy .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Formally, assume a set of tokens $\mathcal{X}$ and a token embedding model $\embmod:\mathcal{X}\rightarrow\mathbb{R ^n$ associating each token with an $n$-dimensional embedding vector.}
elements.sent-2411-063: A randomized mechanism $M: \mathcal{X}\rightarrow\mathcal{Y}$ is said to be $\varepsilon \dx$-private if for any input $x_1, x_2 \in \mathcal{X}$ and any output $y\in \mathcal{Y}$ \cite{feyisetan2020Privacy : \begin{equation}\frac{Pr[M(x_1)=y]}{Pr[M(x_2)=y]}\leq e^{\varepsilon d(x_1, x_2)}\end{equation} Intuitively when $\mathcal{X}$ is a vocabulary of words, the closer two words $x_1$ and $x_2$ are according to the distance metric, the more similar their probabilities to be mapped to a specific output $y$ will be.}
elements.sent-2411-064: In the case of text-to-text sanitization, the output domain is also words and thus $\mathcal{Y =\mathcal{X}$.}
elements.sent-2411-065: Note that Local DP is a special case of $\dx$-privacy where $\forall x_1, x_2\in \mathcal{X : x_1\neq x_2 \Rightarrow d(x_1, x_2) = 1$.}
elements.sent-2411-066: Text-to-text sanitization satisfying $\dx$-privacy takes many forms, \cite{feyisetan2020Privacy, qu2021Natural, yue2021Differential , the specific technique of this work follows that of \cite{feyisetan2020Privacy, qu2021Natural .}}
elements.sent-2411-067: Lines 1 to 3 detail how to sample the noise vector $\eta$: First, the direction of the noise is chosen via a normalized sample from the multivariate normal distribution of dimension $n$, with a 0 mean and the identity matrix as the covariance.
elements.sent-2411-068: Then, the magnitude of the noise is sampled with a gamma distribution of shape $n$ and scale $1/\varepsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-069: By adding the noise to the embedding of $x$, we obtain $e'$ which is a point in $\mathbb{R ^n$ that with high probability does not correspond to any word in $\mathcal{X}$.}
elements.sent-2411-070: We then perform a nearest neighbor search to find the word $z\in \mathcal{X}$ where $\norm{\embmod(z)-e'}$ is minimized, and we consider $z$ as the replacement token of $x$.
elements.sent-2411-071: For a text containing several tokens, the mechanism is applied independently to each token.
elements.sent-2411-072: It is important to note that \cref{alg:dx might output $x' = x$.}
elements.sent-2411-073: In fact, the nearest neighbor of $e'$ could be $x$ itself and in this case the token is not modified.
elements.sent-2411-074: Under classical DP conditions, the $\varepsilon$ parameter defines a privacy/utility tradeoff where greater values of $\varepsilon$ produce smaller amounts of noise and consequently less privacy protection.
elements.sent-2411-075: However, under $\dx$-privacy the meaning of $\varepsilon$ depends on the metric $d$ and $\varepsilon$ is not directly transferable between metrics \cite{feyisetan2020Privacy .}
elements.sent-2411-076: Choosing the value for $\varepsilon$ is not straightforward for end users \cite{nanayakkara2023What .}
elements.sent-2411-077: As such, in this paper we focus on $\dx$-privacy as our main sanitization method and propose an architecture to assess degrees of $\dx$-privacy's impact on the utility of prompts in an attempt for end-users to better navigate the trade-off between privacy and utility.
elements.section-2411-003: The Utility and Privacy Tradeoff Problem in Prompts
elements.sent-2411-078: In this section we motivate the need for more sophisticated means of assessing the privacy-utility tradeoff in prompt sanitization, followed by an overview of our proposed architecture leveraging an SLM to predict the resulting utility.
elements.sent-2411-079: Finally we specify the problem statement against which we shall evaluate the proposed solution.
elements.sent-2411-080: When querying online LLMs, a handful of tasks require the submission of an instruction accompanied by a text on which the task is performed.
elements.sent-2411-081: For example, a summarization task consists in an instruction "Summarize the following text" and a text.
elements.sent-2411-082: Other tasks such as Question Answering where the LLM will answer a question using information contained within a document, or Data Extraction where specific information are extracted from a document, follow the same model.
elements.sent-2411-083: In these cases, the text to be processed might contain sensitive components such as PII or trade secrets that the user or its employer does not want to be revealed to the LLM's host.
elements.sent-2411-084: For this reason, the user (or its employer on the user's behalf) will resort to sanitizing the prompt.
elements.sent-2411-085: This may be achieved with manual sanitization at a heavy burden and cost to users~\cite{gordon2013mra , or they can leverage automatic methods (See \cref{sec:sota for an overview of existing approaches).}}
elements.sent-2411-086: Because this process modifies the content of the prompt, it affects the performances of the downstream LLM task performed on said prompt.
elements.sent-2411-087: The user understands the impact of sanitization and seeks a balance between privacy and result quality.
elements.sent-2411-088: They are willing to compromise slightly on quality for the sake of privacy, provided the results remain above a certain usability threshold.
elements.sent-2411-089: Simultaneously, they are willing to compromise on privacy to some extent to achieve this balance, but not below a chosen privacy level.
elements.sent-2411-090: If the desired utility cannot be achieved within their expected privacy parameters, they would prefer to conserve resources and refrain from sending the prompt altogether.
elements.sent-2411-091: One might consider a straightforward solution: sending the same prompt multiple times, each with a different sanitization method or varying parameters, until the desired result quality is achieved or the privacy level becomes unacceptably low.
elements.sent-2411-092: However, this approach is inefficient in terms of computing and financial resources as only the final result is useful to the user.
elements.sent-2411-094: As \cite{dwork2008differential puts it simply, if DP noise is centered around the original point, repeated sanitized queries of the same text can be averaged, effectively canceling the noise out.}
elements.sent-2411-095: Hence, given a single text we need a way to choose an appropriate sanitized version of the text before sending it.
elements.sent-2411-096: The chosen version should have a high probability of producing a result of adequate quality.
elements.sent-2411-097: For an unmodified prompt, the quality of the result depends on the task, the specific prompt and the chosen LLM.
elements.sent-2411-098: Sanitizing the prompt introduces additional challenging factors such as the sanitization process itself that may not preserve the semantic similarity and the relevance of the sensitive information (to be sanitized) included in the text.
elements.sent-2411-099: In the case of $\dx$-privacy, the epsilon parameter governs privacy by introducing different levels of noise to the text.
elements.sent-2411-100: In addition, the values of $\varepsilon$ are not directly comparable to those in classical DP as they depend on the chosen distance metric.
elements.sent-2411-101: Overall, predicting the performance of an LLM on a given sanitized text is challenging.\.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[clip, trim=0 9.5cm 11cm 0, width=0.8\columnwidth]{Archi.pdf} \caption{Architecture of Text Sanitization Utility estimation} \label{fig:architecture}
elements.sent-2411-102: The architecture we consider is illustrated in \cref{fig:architecture .}
elements.sent-2411-103: Locally (left-hand side of the figure), the user has a document and unlimited access to an SLM.
elements.sent-2411-104: They seek to query an LLM (right-hand side of the figure) hosted online.
elements.sent-2411-105: We define an SLM as a model capable of running on the user-side, either on the user's own machine or on a company-hosted machine in the setting of an employer protecting its employees' prompts.
elements.sent-2411-106: This SLM is in contrast to the extreme size of current online LLMs with an estimated 1.8 trillion parameters for ChatGPT-4\footnote{\url{https://the-decoder.com/gpt-4-architecture-datasets-costs-and-more-leaked/ .}}
elements.sent-2411-107: To this end, we consider SLMs with at most one billion parameters (For example, a 32-bit precision model with 1B parameters necessitates approximately 4GB of VRAM).
elements.sent-2411-108: They can be either general-purpose or fine-tuned for a specific task.
elements.sent-2411-109: While fine-tuned models might lead to performances close the one of the general purpose LLM, they are not task-agnostic and several SLMs might be needed to cover a full range of language model tasks.
elements.sent-2411-110: This architecture does not depend on the precise sanitization process employed and can be used for other methods than $\dx$-privacy.
elements.sent-2411-111: Our architecture targets tasks where the prompts contain a general instruction which does not need privacy, and an associated document $p$ which might contain sensitive elements.
elements.sent-2411-112: The user can perform automatic sanitization of $p$ locally as many times as needed and obtain the modified version $p_\varepsilon$ (See \cref{fig:architecture ).}
elements.sent-2411-113: Furthermore, they have unlimited access to the local SLM, which execution is considered cheap in terms of computing resources.
elements.sent-2411-114: They can submit $p$ to the SLM and obtain a result $r^{SLM}$.
elements.sent-2411-115: They can do the same with $p_\varepsilon$ and obtain $r_\varepsilon^{SLM}$.
elements.sent-2411-116: Finally, they will submit $p_\varepsilon$ to the online LLM and receive the result $r_\varepsilon^{LLM}$.
elements.sent-2411-117: In our threat model, the host of the online LLM to be queried is honest but curious.
elements.sent-2411-118: They deliver the service but collects data from received prompts.
elements.sent-2411-119: The user's local environment as well as the communication between the user and the online LLM are considered trusted.
elements.sent-2411-120: Problem statement Considering the resource-constrained privacy preserving LLM interaction architecture, the computing model and the threat model defined above, can we predict the performance of an LLM? Put simply, can a trusted, smaller language model help users balance privacy and utility when sharing a document with a larger LLM?.
elements.sent-2411-121: In this experimental section, we shall first outline the configuration of our experiments, followed by our evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-122: This begins with an evaluation of the generative output performance between the SLM and LLM on the original and sanitized texts.
elements.sent-2411-123: We evaluate the capability for the SLM's behavior to estimate the expected LLM utility.
elements.sent-2411-124: Finally we investigate the semantic preservation property of $\dx$-privacy.
elements.sent-2411-125: Our experiment code is available online\footnotemark[3].
elements.sent-2411-126: In our experiments, we target a summarization task where texts of variable length are to be sent to the LLM to receive results of limited length.
elements.sent-2411-127: This task was chosen for being a popular document-driven language model task with different open SLMs available online for experiment.
elements.sent-2411-128: SLMs: We use three different SLMs to summarize text, namely BART in its large-cnn variant\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/facebook/bart-large-cnn and commit version 37f520f, TinyLlama\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/TinyLlama/TinyLlama-1.1B-Chat-v1.0 in its commit version fe8a4ea and T5 Small\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/Falconsai/text_summarization/ in its commit version 6e505f9.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-129: While BART and T5 are models specifically tuned for summarization, TinyLlama is a general-purpose chat model.
elements.sent-2411-130: We include one general-purpose SLM for a direct comparison with the general-purpose LLM, and two SLMs fine-tuned for summarization to give them the greatest opportunity to approach the performance of the LLM on this task.
elements.sent-2411-131: LLM: For the target LLM to be queried we use the Llama-3-8B-Instruct model in its e5e23bb commit version\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct .}}
elements.sent-2411-132: We opt to host and run the model ourselves to lower the experiment cost and improve reproducibility, as online service offerings iteratively change.
elements.sent-2411-133: While this model's size is smaller than the trillion-parameters models available online, the size asymmetry between Llama-3 and our SLMs is preserved with T5, BART and TinyLlama being respectively 132x, 20x and 7x smaller in terms of number of parameters.
elements.sent-2411-134: Dataset: We use the Multi News dataset \cite{fabbri2019Multinewsa in its 38cb206 commit version\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/datasets/alexfabbri/multi_news .}}}
elements.sent-2411-135: This dataset contains news articles from multiple sources associated with human-written summaries.
elements.sent-2411-136: The full dataset is concatenated from its train, validation and test subsets and cleaned to the best of our ability by removing unrelated headers, faulty entries, and other abnormalities (See our code release\footnotemark[3] for full description of data preparation).
elements.sent-2411-137: As our SLMs only support inputs of at most 1024 tokens, for a fair comparison we only select texts of this length, with the final evaluation dataset containing 11,121 documents.
elements.sent-2411-138: Sanitization: We implemented $\dx$-privacy for text as described in \cref{subsec:santize .}
elements.sent-2411-139: With this technique all 11,121 samples of our evaluation dataset are sanitized with $\varepsilon$ values ranging from $1$ to $100$, using the internal token vocabulary of our BART model as the reference embedding space.
elements.sent-2411-140: Utility Metric: We measure the quality of a given summarization as the cosine similarity between the original text and the summary using the all-mpnet-base-v2 model of the Sentence Transformer package\footnote{\url{https://www.sbert.net/docs/sentence_transformer/pretrained_models.html .}}
elements.subsection-2411-007: SLM vs LLM Output Generation with Original Texts
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{subfigure}{.5\columnwidth} \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{BART-OGtextVSgenSummary.pdf} \caption{BART} \label{fig:BART-OGtextVSgenSummary} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.5\columnwidth} \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{OGtextVSLlamagenSummary.pdf} \caption{Llama-3} \label{fig:Llama-OGtextVSgenSummary} \end{subfigure} \caption{Similarity between the original text and a summary generated from the text.} \label{fig:OGtextVSgenSummary}
elements.sent-2411-141: We first compare the performances of our SLM and LLM models on the original texts.
elements.sent-2411-142: Illustrated in \cref{fig:OGtextVSgenSummary is the cosine similarity between the original text and a summary generated from this text, either by BART (\cref{fig:BART-OGtextVSgenSummary ) or Llama-3 (\cref{fig:Llama-OGtextVSgenSummary ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-143: For a fair comparison we set the maximum output length at 142 tokens for both models.
elements.sent-2411-144: The Llama-3 model was instructed with: ``Your task is to summarize your input text.".
elements.sent-2411-145: As expected, the results show that the larger model of Llama-3 produces better results than BART, obtaining a cosine similarity over $0.77$ for 90\% of the samples with an average of $0.86$.
elements.sent-2411-146: BART leads to a cosine similarity over $0.67$ for 90\% of the samples with an average of $0.79$.
elements.sent-2411-147: This experiment shows that while BART is specifically specialized in summarization, the general-purpose Llama-3 still yields greater performance, justifying the use of bigger models.
elements.subsection-2411-008: Impact of Text Sanitization on Generation Performance
elements.figure-2411-003: \begin{subfigure}{.5\columnwidth} \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{LLAMA-OGtextVSLlamanoisygenSummary001.pdf} \caption{$\varepsilon=1$} \label{fig:LLAMA-OGtextVSnoisygenSummary1} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.5\columnwidth} \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{LLAMA-OGtextVSLlamanoisygenSummary025.pdf} \caption{$\varepsilon=25$} \label{fig:LLAMA-OGtextVSnoisygenSummary25} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.5\columnwidth} \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{LLAMA-OGtextVSLlamanoisygenSummary050.pdf} \caption{$\varepsilon=50$} \label{fig:LLAMA-OGtextVSnoisygenSummary50} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.5\columnwidth} \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{LLAMA-OGtextVSLlamanoisygenSummary075.pdf} \caption{$\varepsilon=75$} \label{fig:LLAMA-OGtextVSnoisygenSummary75} \end{subfigure} \caption{Similarity between the original text and a summary generated from the sanitized version of the text with Llama-3} \label{fig:LLAMA-OGtextVSnoisygenSummary}
elements.sent-2411-148: As we have previously explained, modifications performed by sanitizing the text will impact the utility of said texts.
elements.sent-2411-149: In \cref{fig:LLAMA-OGtextVSnoisygenSummary , we show the cosine similarity between a text and a summary generated by Llama-3 from a sanitized version of this text, for different values of $\varepsilon$.}
elements.sent-2411-150: This figure showcases the very large range of utility one can expect depending on the text for a given $\varepsilon$ value.
elements.sent-2411-151: At $\varepsilon=50$, 20\% of the texts lead to a similarity under 0.55 while another 20\% lead to a similarity greater than 0.81.
elements.sent-2411-152: From these results, there is a clear need for a means to estimate the utility of a given sanitized text before committing to using the LLM, thereby avoiding unnecessary costs of the LLM service on otherwise unusable text.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.60\columnwidth]{SimLossesMean.pdf} \caption{Text Similarity losses of different models compared with the percentage of tokens changed, depending on $\varepsilon$.} \label{fig:SimLossesMean}
elements.sent-2411-153: We now turn our attention to the multiple SLMs under evaluations, with \cref{fig:SimLossesMean providing an overview of the performances of each model as a function of $\varepsilon$.}
elements.sent-2411-154: In this chart "Avg \% of tokens changed" represents the percentage of tokens that have been modified per text, averaged over all texts.
elements.sent-2411-155: Then, "Avg Text Similarity Loss" is computed by averaging the value of $1 - Similarity\left(\text{Original text , \text{Sanitized text}\right)$ for all texts.}
elements.sent-2411-156: Finally, the summarization similarity loss for each model expresses the difference in similarity between the original summarization and the summarization on sanitized text and is the average for all texts of: Summ.. sim.
elements.sent-2411-157: From $\varepsilon=1$ to $30$, all models have equally poor performance when generating summaries from highly sanitized texts, as reflected in the summarization similarity losses ranging from $0.69$ (T5) to $0.78$ (TinyLlama).
elements.sent-2411-158: We hypothesize this is partially attributed to the average $97$\% of tokens between these two values of $\varepsilon$ being modified per text, of which we shall further investigate in the next subsection.

elements.sent-2411-205: This section details works related to our own in an effort to contextualize our findings in the wider research landscape.
elements.sent-2411-206: This section will first look at related works in the general area of text sanitization, before exploring specific works attempting to improve the privacy of prompts submitted to LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-207: It is known that extensive labor is required for humans to manually redact textual documents \cite{gordon2013mra .}

elements.sent-2411-242: To conclude, this paper studies the utility impact of text sanitization when used on LLM prompts.
elements.sent-2411-243: Our goal is to conserve resources -both monetary for users and computational for the LLMs host- by determining when post-sanitization of prompts reduces their effectiveness for generating meaningful LLM responses.

elements.sent-2411-262: It can be applied on the entire text or on specific, privacy-exposing elements detected by Named Entity Recognition~\cite{chen2023Hide, chong2024Casper, papadopoulou2023Neural .}
elements.sent-2411-263: All these sanitization techniques involve altering the text to various degrees which can reduce its utility~\cite{zhang2024No .}
elements.sent-2411-264: If said text is a prompt intended to be sent to an LLM for a task (e.g., summarization), the result might suffer in correctness, accuracy and/or coherence compared to what the LLM would have produced with the unaltered prompt.
elements.sent-2411-265: Excessive alteration can even lead to totally useless results which are wasted resources for the provider and unnecessary expenses for the user when the LLM usage incurs a fee.

elements.sent-2411-267: To prevent employees from inadvertently submitting sensitive or Personally Identifiable Information (PII) into prompts, a company aims to sanitize user prompts before they are sent to the LLM provider.
elements.sent-2411-268: However, an excessive loss in utility caused by the sanitization is undesirable from the company's perspective as it incurs costs for every prompt sent to the LLM provider.
elements.sent-2411-269: In this context, we investigated the possibility of locally predicting the LLM's performance on a given sanitized prompt.
elements.sent-2411-270: This way, we can avoid sending excessively sanitized prompts which will produce meaningless results and waste resource.
elements.sent-2411-271: Our prediction is assisted by running a Small Language Model (SLM) at the edge, either on the employee’s device or within the company's infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-272: Although less powerful than LLMs, the SLM is trusted and tasked to assess the quality of a sanitized prompt before sending it to an online LLM.
elements.sent-2411-273: If the SLM determines that the sanitized prompt would produce meaningless results, the sanitization can be adjusted (e.g., applying a lighter version if permitted by company policy).
elements.sent-2411-274: The first advantage is that this prevents multiple sanitized versions of the same prompt from being sent to the LLM in search of a satisfactory result—a process that could compromise privacy.
elements.sent-2411-275: Additionally, if no acceptable sanitization is possible within policy constraints, the prompt is not sent to the LLM, avoiding resource waste, and the SLM handles the task itself using the unaltered prompt.
elements.sent-2411-276: This approach aims to offer the company and its employees the best value for their money by balancing privacy, utility, and financial resources.

elements.sent-2411-280: Overall, in this paper we make the following contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-281: We propose an architecture that locally evaluates the quality of a prompt sanitized using a privacy-preserving text sanitization method.
elements.sent-2411-282: This evaluation occurs before sending the prompt to an LLM, aiming to prevent resource waste if the sanitization has degraded the prompt's utility to the point where satisfactory results are unlikely.
elements.sent-2411-283: We refer to this architecture as the utility assessor.
elements.sent-2411-284: We empirically evaluate the utility of prompts sanitized by $\dx$-privacy, a word-level Differential Privacy method leveraging the Multidimensional Laplace Mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-285: Our evaluation targets a summarization task performed on a publicly available dataset, leveraging three SLMs and two LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-286: With lower amounts of noise, the same $\varepsilon$ value may provide no utility when applied on one prompt while offering very high utility when applied on another as word-level DP can alter semantics to varying degrees.
elements.sent-2411-287: Hence, predicting the utility of sanitized prompts requires an advanced technique such as our utility assessor.
elements.sent-2411-288: In order to better predict the utility of a prompt ---wether it would waste resources or not---, we rely on several numerical features beyond just the $\varepsilon$ value, available through our utility assessor.
elements.sent-2411-289: This includes semantic similarities involving the unaltered prompt and the SLM's results on the unaltered and sanitized prompts.
elements.sent-2411-290: Using these features we trained a regressor to predict the performance of an LLM on a given sanitized prompt.
elements.sent-2411-291: Compared to the use of $\varepsilon$ alone, we show that using the utility assessor's features enables saving the computing and monetary resources of up to 12\% of the prompts that were previously wasted.
elements.sent-2411-292: We identify an issue in Feyisetan et al.~\cite{feyisetan2020Privacy which, to the best of our knowledge, is one of the most cited paper on Differential Privacy applied on text through the Multidimensional Laplace Mechanism.}

elements.sent-2411-304: Vast quantities of text are used to progressively learn the representation of tokens (i.e., a word or a word segment) as $n$-dimensional vectors called embeddings where the distances in the embedding space express the semantic difference between tokens.

elements.sent-2411-306: A randomized mechanism $M: \mathcal{X}\rightarrow\mathcal{Y}$ is said to be $\varepsilon \dx$-private if for any input $x_1, x_2 \in \mathcal{X}$ and any output $y\in \mathcal{Y}$ \cite{feyisetan2020Privacy : \begin{equation}\frac{Pr[M(x_1)=y]}{Pr[M(x_2)=y]}\leq e^{\varepsilon d(x_1, x_2)}\end{equation} Intuitively when $\mathcal{X}$ is a set of words, the closer two words $x_1$ and $x_2$ are in the embedding space according to the distance metric, the more similar their probabilities to be mapped to a specific output $y$ will be.}

elements.section-2411-008: Utility and Privacy Trade-off for Prompts
elements.sent-2411-331: For example, a summarization task may consist in an instruction "Summarize the following text" and a text.
elements.sent-2411-332: Other tasks follow the same model, such as Question Answering where the LLM will answer a question using information contained within a textual document, or Data Extraction where specific information are extracted from a document.
elements.sent-2411-333: In these cases, the text to be processed might contain sensitive elements such as PII or trade secrets that the user or its employer does not want to be revealed to the LLM's provider.
elements.sent-2411-334: For this reason, the user (or its employer on the user's behalf) will resort to sanitizing the associated text.
elements.sent-2411-335: For clarity, in the rest of this paper we refer to this associated text as the prompt as we consider that the instruction mentionned above does not require privacy protection.
elements.sent-2411-336: Sanitizing the prompt can be achieved with manual sanitization at a heavy burden and cost to users~\cite{gordon2013mra , or one can leverage automatic methods.}
elements.sent-2411-337: In particular, we consider the differentially-private text sanitization method described in the previous section (See \cref{sec:sota for an overview of other sanitization approaches).}
elements.sent-2411-338: Because sanitization modifies the content of the prompt, it affects the performances of the downstream LLM task performed on said prompt.
elements.sent-2411-339: To illustrate the extent to which a text is altered by differentially-private text sanitization, consider the following text sample: \begin{textsample Emily Carter, born on April 12, 1990, resides at 482 Maple Street, Springfield, IL, and her Social Security Number is 123-45-6789.}
elements.sent-2411-340: Her credit card number, 4111-1111-1111-1111, expires in 06/27, and her personal email is emily.carter90@email.com.
elements.sent-2411-341: We notice that the result lacks proper grammar and punctuation.
elements.sent-2411-342: Also, while the text conserves its meaning to some extent (i.e., presenting a person), some information is partially altered such as the social security number, while others like the name are entirely modified.
elements.sent-2411-343: Decreasing $\varepsilon$ achieves better privacy but also lowers utility.
elements.sent-2411-344: Below is a sanitized version\footnote{Included as an image to avoid formatting issues of the text using $\varepsilon=35$: \begin{textsample \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{garbageText.pdf \end{textsample The text has lost all of its meaning, grammar and punctuation.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-345: Some characters are not even English and others cannot be printed.
elements.sent-2411-346: Such text deterioration is expected given \cref{alg:dx .}
elements.sent-2411-347: Indeed, considering a token as a point of an $n$-dimensional vector space, the noise added by the mechanism can point towards any direction, with its magnitude scaled by $\varepsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-348: Consequently, a sufficiently low value of $\varepsilon$ potentially leads to a perturbed point relatively far from the original point.
elements.sent-2411-349: Because embedding models are trained on vast amounts of textual data, they contain a highly diverse set of tokens that can be chosen as output by the mechanism, hence the non-printable and non-English characters.
elements.sent-2411-350: Any language model task computed on such sanitized prompt will suffer in performance.
elements.sent-2411-351: A user looking for satisfactory performance of an LLM on such task might consider a simple yet insecure solution: sending the same prompt multiple times, each time decreasing the sanitization degree.
elements.sent-2411-352: This can be repeated until the desired result quality is achieved or the privacy level becomes unacceptably low.
elements.sent-2411-353: First, this approach is inefficient in terms of computing and financial resources as only the last query result is useful to the user.
elements.sent-2411-354: Second, it poses privacy risks: As \cite{dwork2008differential puts it simply, repeated sanitized queries of the same prompt can be averaged, effectively canceling the noise out and revealing the original input.}
elements.sent-2411-355: Thus, the user is in need of a solution to anticipate the performance of the LLM before actually sending the prompt.
elements.sent-2411-356: Without sanitization, such performance is usually influenced by the exact task and the LLM used for the task.
elements.sent-2411-357: The sanitization introduces another factor, mainly driven by $\varepsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-358: However, as mentioned in \cref{sec:backDX , $\varepsilon$ values are not similar to classic DP making it more challenging to select an appropriate value.}
elements.sent-2411-359: For example, the last text sample above is sanitized using $\varepsilon=35$, a value that would be considered non-private in classic DP, while it is still clearly too private to get any utility.
elements.sent-2411-360: Moreover, the same $\varepsilon$ value can affect different texts to various degrees as we will see in \cref{sec:expe .}
elements.sent-2411-361: Overall, predicting the performance of an LLM on a given sanitized prompt before it is sent is challenging.
elements.sent-2411-362: We propose a solution architecture where a user benefits from a trusted SLM to assess locally the utility of a given sanitization of a prompt.
elements.sent-2411-363: As the prompt is going to be sent to an LLM for inference, this assessment enables to anticipate the LLM's performance and avoid wasting its resources if the expected result will not meet a desired quality.
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[clip, trim=0 10.5cm 19.1cm 0, width=\linewidth]{Archi.pdf} \caption{Utility Assessor Architecture. $p$ represents the unaltered prompt. $p_\varepsilon$ denotes the sanitized version of the prompt. $r^{SLM}$ and $r^{SLM}_\varepsilon$ designate the SLM's result on the unaltered and sanitized prompts, respectively. $r^{LLM}_\varepsilon$ identifies the LLM's result on the sanitized prompt.} \label{fig:architecture}
elements.sent-2411-364: It deals with language model tasks where the prompt $p$ includes a document which might contain sensitive elements.. Computing Model.
elements.sent-2411-365: Locally (left-hand side of the figure), the user can apply a Sanitization procedure to $p$, parametrized by $\varepsilon$, to obtain its sanitized version $p_\varepsilon$ (Step 1).
elements.sent-2411-366: They have access to an SLM to run the language model task on $p$ and on $p_\varepsilon$ to obtain the associated results $r^{SLM}$ and $r^{SLM _\varepsilon$ (Step 2 and 3).}
elements.sent-2411-367: They also have a Utility Estimator procedure which evaluates the quality of the SLM's results (Step 4).
elements.sent-2411-368: Finally, when a given $p_\varepsilon$ is assessed to satisfies both privacy and utility criterion, it is sent to the LLM hosted online (right-hand side of the figure) to perform the language model task (Step 5).
elements.sent-2411-369: Note that all steps from 1 to 4 included can be repeated as many times as needed, with different values of $\varepsilon$ until the architecture predicts the expected quality of Step 5 will be high enough or until the privacy level becomes unacceptably low (e.g., per company policy).
elements.sent-2411-370: In this latter case, we cannot both satisfy privacy preservation and adequate performance but the user can nonetheless benefit from $r^{SLM}$ as the result.
elements.sent-2411-371: We define an SLM as a model capable of running on the edge, either on the user's own machine or on a machine hosted by their company.
elements.sent-2411-372: The large size of online LLMs, such as ChatGPT-4 with an estimated 1.8 trillion parameters\footnote{\url{https://the-decoder.com/gpt-4-architecture-datasets-costs-and-more-leaked/ , makes them impractical for local hosting.}}
elements.sent-2411-373: In contrast, we consider SLMs with at most one billion parameters (such model with 32-bit precision necessitates approximately 4GB of VRAM).
elements.sent-2411-374: As such, its execution is considered cheap in terms of computing resources.
elements.sent-2411-375: The SLMs we consider can be either general-purpose or fine-tuned for a specific task.
elements.sent-2411-376: Fine-tuned models might have better performances on a task, and thus might improve the quality of the prediction of the LLM's performance on the same task.
elements.sent-2411-377: However, they are not task-agnostic and several SLMs of this type might be needed to cover a full range of language model tasks.
elements.sent-2411-378: We tested both types in our experiments (See \cref{sec:expe ).}
elements.sent-2411-379: The Utility Estimator is task-dependent and judges the quality of $p_\varepsilon$, $r^{SLM}$ and $r^{SLM _\varepsilon$.}
elements.sent-2411-380: For example, semantic similarity between $p$ and $r^{SLM _\varepsilon$ can be used to assess the proximity of a text and its summary in a summarization task.}
elements.sent-2411-381: In a question-answering task, we can compare $r^{SLM}$ and $r^{SLM _\varepsilon$ together with semantic similarity or F1-score.}
elements.sent-2411-382: Similarities, F1-Score and any other quality metric are considered as features and are subsequently used in a regression task to predict the utility of $r^{LLM _\varepsilon$.}
elements.sent-2411-383: Such regressor can be trained on the flow: while sanitized prompts are being sent to the LLM and answers are received, input features can be extracted from $p_\varepsilon$, $r^{SLM}$ and $r^{SLM _\varepsilon$, while the target feature can be derived from $r^{LLM _\varepsilon$.. Threat Model.}}
elements.sent-2411-384: The provider of the online LLM is honest but curious.
elements.sent-2411-385: Problem statement Considering the utility assessor architecture, the computing model and the threat model defined above, can we predict the performance of an LLM? Put simply, can a trusted, smaller language model help users balance privacy and utility when sharing a document with a larger language model?.
elements.sent-2411-386: In this experimental section, we shall first outline the configuration of our experiments.
elements.sent-2411-387: Then, we evaluate the performance of the tested language models on unaltered prompts.
elements.sent-2411-388: Next, we show the enormous impact of prompt sanitization on the performance of all the language models.
elements.sent-2411-389: We then evaluate the capability of our utility assessor architecture to estimate the expected LLM performance on sanitized prompts.
elements.sent-2411-390: In our experiments, we target a summarization task where an accurate summary of a text has to be produced by the language model in a limited number of tokens.
elements.sent-2411-391: It is a popular document-driven language model task with many openly available SLMs for experiment.
elements.sent-2411-392: We chose this particular task as it is notably relevant in a work setting: summarizing long e-mails and reports is a desirable feature for employees for which the company itself would want to impose privacy protection as these documents may contain confidential information or PIIs.
elements.sent-2411-393: SLM: We use three different SLMs to summarize text, namely T5 Small\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/Falconsai/text_summarization/ (60 Million parameters), bart-large-cnn\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/facebook/bart-large-cnn (400 Million parameters) and Llama 3.2 in its 1 Billion parameters version\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-394: These models were chosen for their popularity on the huggingface platform\footnote{T5, Bart and Llama3.2-1B have respectively 1M, 88M and 16M all-time downloads.}
elements.sent-2411-395: While T5 and BART are specifically tuned for summarization, Llama3.2-1B is a general-purpose chat model.
elements.sent-2411-396: LLM: For the target LLM to be queried we tested two models: Llama-3-8B-Instruct\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct and Gemini-2.0-flash\footnote{\url{https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/models/gemini .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-397: We choose to host and run the first model ourselves to reduce experiment costs and enhance reproducibility, as online service offerings evolve over time.
elements.sent-2411-398: While Llama-3 is smaller than trillion-parameter models available online, the size gap between Llama-3 and our SLMs remains significant: T5, BART, and Llama3.2-1B are respectively 132x, 20x, and 7x smaller in terms of parameter count.
elements.sent-2411-399: We also include Gemini to involve a recent online large language model, though the exact number of parameters for Gemini-2.0-Flash is unknown.
elements.sent-2411-400: Dataset: We use the Multi News dataset \cite{fabbri2019Multinewsa which contains news articles from multiple sources associated with human-written summaries.}
elements.sent-2411-401: We concatenated its train, validation and test subsets and cleaned it to the best of our ability by removing unrelated headers, faulty entries, and other abnormalities (See our code release\footnotemark[3] for full description of data preparation).
elements.sent-2411-402: As some of our SLMs only support inputs of at most 1024 tokens, for a fair comparison we only select texts of this length, with the final evaluation dataset containing 11,121 documents.
elements.sent-2411-403: For all experiment we randomly sample 1,500 elements from this dataset.
elements.sent-2411-404: Sanitization: We implemented $\dx$-privacy sanitization for text as described in \cref{subsec:santize .}
elements.sent-2411-405: This mechanism relies on an embedding model $\embmod$ to convert text into $n$-dimensional vectors.
elements.sent-2411-406: While word embedding models such as GloVe~\cite{pennington2014GloVe and word2vec~\cite{word2vec are commonly used for this task~\cite{fernandes2019Generalised, yue2021Differential, feyisetan2020Privacy, asghar2024dxprivacy , they have a significant limitation: words not present in their vocabulary cannot be sanitized.}}}
elements.sent-2411-407: Simply removing such words is not an option either, as the privacy guarantees of the sanitization mechanism apply only to strings of the same length~\cite{feyisetan2020Privacy, mattern2022limits .}
elements.sent-2411-408: This issue is particularly problematic because out-of-vocabulary words—those not encountered during the embedding model’s training—are often rare names of people or places, which are precisely the types of sensitive information that require protection.
elements.sent-2411-409: For this reason, we opted for token embedding models from two of our language models, namely bart-large-cnn (50,264 tokens, 1024 dimensions) and Llama-3-8B-Instruct (128,256 tokens, 4096 dimensions).
elements.sent-2411-410: In our approach, text is first tokenized, and the corresponding vector for each token is used for sanitization.
elements.sent-2411-411: Summarization: We requested models to limit their output to 142 tokens as it is the default value for the bart-large-cnn model and we found it to be a suitable choice for all models.
elements.sent-2411-412: General-purpose models were driven to summarize using specific prompts which can be found in the code release\hyperref[footnoteCodeRepo]{\textsuperscript{\ref{footnoteCodeRepo .}}}
elements.sent-2411-413: Utility Estimator: We assess summary quality using the all-mpnet-base-v2 model from the Sentence Transformer package\footnote{\url{https://www.sbert.net/docs/sentence_transformer/pretrained_models.html .}}
elements.sent-2411-414: This model generates one embedding vector for an entire text, and the similarity between two texts is measured as the cosine similarity between their embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-415: Similarity ranges from -1 to 1: values near -1 indicate opposing texts, values around 0 suggest no correlation (orthogonal texts), and values close to 1 signify high similarity.
elements.sent-2411-416: While we have also experimented with two other text similarity models, namely gte-large-en-v1.5\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/Alibaba-NLP/gte-large-en-v1.5 and modernbert-embed-base\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/nomic-ai/modernbert-embed-base , the result were not satisfactory as they designate overly sanitized texts (i.e., $\varepsilon=1$) as similar to genuine English text.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-417: The details of the regression task will be described in \cref{subsec:predi .}

elements.sent-2411-523: In this section we mention related works for prompts privacy and how they navigate the privacy-utility trade-off.
elements.sent-2411-524: The authors propose two anonymization schemes: generative and label-based.
elements.sent-2411-525: The generative scheme features a training phase where a corpus of prompts is anonymized by an online LLM requested to replace particular private entities with ``random words''~\cite{chen2023Hide .}
elements.sent-2411-526: Then, a smaller language model is tasked to anonymize in the same manner by providing it both the unaltered prompts and the corresponding LLM anonymized prompt.
elements.sent-2411-527: By replacing entities with random words or placeholders, both schemes do not preserve semantics of the PII.
elements.sent-2411-528: Also for the generative scheme it is unclear to what extent the smaller language model will be able to anonymize entities not seen during training.
elements.sent-2411-529: The label-based scheme is dependent on the NER models to correctly detect entities for sanitization.
elements.sent-2411-530: Finally, we are not aware of a means to navigate the privacy-utility trade-off similar to the $\varepsilon$ value in DP.
elements.sent-2411-531: In our work, we use a text sanitization mechanism where the semantic is preserved through related tokens and the privacy-utility trade-off is easily controllable through our utility assessor architecture.
elements.sent-2411-532: The authors of \cite{shen2024fire propose a solution to sanitize the prompts of LLMs.}

elements.sent-2411-534: However, we argue that by specifically targeting low importance words the semantic of the prompt is indeed preserved, but the arguably more significant case of words having both a high importance score and a high privacy sensitivity is not considered.
elements.sent-2411-535: In our work, all words of a text are sanitized independently and thus the privacy guarantees of the sanitization do not depend on the correct detection of sensitive terms.
elements.sent-2411-536: A Small Language model is locally hosted and the user wants to query an online and untrusted LLM to perform a text continuation task on a sensitive document.

elements.sent-2411-541: To conclude, this paper studies the utility impact of text sanitization when applied on Large Language Model (LLM) prompts.
elements.sent-2411-542: Our goal is to conserve resources ---both monetary for users and computational for the LLM provider--- by predicting when sanitized prompts have lost their effectiveness for generating meaningful LLM responses.
elements.sent-2411-543: This is particularly relevant in a professional context where a company benefits from employees using LLMs to increase productivity, but at the same time wants to avoid leakage of trade secrets and company data in the prompts.
elements.sent-2411-544: To do so they will resort to automatic sanitization methods.
elements.sent-2411-545: We focus on a Differential Privacy (DP) framework applied on text called $\dx$-privacy.
elements.sent-2411-546: We show that selecting the right sanitization degree for this mechanism is hard for end-user as it involves $\varepsilon$ values atypical in DP and cannot be set once and for all as it affects each prompt differently.
elements.sent-2411-547: We propose a utility assessor architecture including a locally trusted small language model (SLM) to inform users about the utility/privacy trade-off before engaging with the LLM.
elements.sent-2411-548: We explore the potential of this architecture by conducting a summarization task with a publicly available dataset, two LLMs, and three SLMs of different capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-549: Our experiments are clear on the heavy and unintuitive impact sanitization can have on the utility of prompts: In one of our setting, both $\varepsilon=1$ and $\varepsilon=200$ lead to the same extremely poor results.
elements.sent-2411-550: In another setting, two prompts sanitized with the same $\varepsilon$ value can give very low or very high utility.
elements.sent-2411-551: Nonetheless, we show that the performance trends between SLMs and LLMs are similar.
elements.sent-2411-552: By leveraging numerical features available via our utility assessor, we train a regression model and confirm the benefit of our architecture where up to 12\% of the prompts previously wasting resources are now saved.
elements.sent-2411-553: In a company setting this is 12\% of all API calls saved, while precious computing resources are saved on the LLM's provider side.
elements.sent-2411-554: While our experiments are limited to a summarization task, we believe this task to be crucial in the work context that we consider: summarizing long reports or professional document is a desired capability for productivity, but they are the same document which the company wants to protect.
elements.sent-2411-555: Also, our work is targeted on a specific sanitization technique, namely $\dx$-privacy for text via the Multidimensional Laplace Mechanism, chosen because it relies on the well-established Differential Privacy (DP) framework.
elements.sent-2411-556: We leave the study of other language modeling tasks on the output of additional sanitization techniques for future works.
elements.sent-2411-557: We aim to encourage the community to explore in greater depth the relationship between privacy preservation, utility and resource inefficiency.
elements.sent-2411-558: Finally, additional experiments shed light on an issue in a popular work about text sanitization based on $\dx$-privacy~\cite{feyisetan2020Privacy .}
elements.sent-2411-559: We reveal that a potential performance-driven implementation decision has a strong impact on the results shown in~\cite{feyisetan2020Privacy .}
elements.sent-2411-560: However, no such decision is disclosed in the paper to the best of our knowledge, and our implementation strictly following their definition has a severely degraded privacy protection in the same experiment settings.
elements.sent-2411-561: We encourage reproducibility and release the code of our experiments\hyperref[footnoteCodeRepo]{\textsuperscript{\ref{footnoteCodeRepo .. \bibliographystyle{ACM-Reference-Format.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-562: While textual prompts can be sanitized using Differential Privacy, we show that it is difficult to anticipate the performance of an LLM on such sanitized prompt.
elements.sent-2411-563: Poor performance has clear monetary consequences for LLM services charging on a pay-per-use model as well as great amount of computing resources wasted.
elements.sent-2411-564: To this end, we propose a middleware architecture leveraging a Small Language Model to predict the utility of a given sanitized prompt before it is sent to the LLM.
elements.sent-2411-565: We experimented on a summarization task and a translation task to show that our architecture helps prevent such resource waste for up to 20\% of the prompts.
elements.sent-2411-566: During our study, we also reproduced experiments from one of the most cited paper on text sanitization using DP and show that a potential performance-driven implementation choice dramatically changes the output while not being explicitly acknowledged in the paper.
elements.sent-2411-567: In addition to data being revealed to the provider itself, prompts may also be used to improve LLMs, which in turn are known to memorize their training data and may disclose them when subsequently queried by other users~\cite{carlini2019secret .}
elements.sent-2411-568: While textual prompts can be manually redacted and sanitized by human experts, such process is time consuming and often not practical in several applications \cite{gordon2013mra .}

elements.sent-2411-571: Throughout this work, utility denotes the degree to which an input (e.g., a prompt) enables high-quality results in a particular computation (e.g., a language model task), as measured by an appropriate evaluation metric (e.g., accuracy or semantic similarity).

elements.sent-2411-573: To prevent employees from inadvertently submitting sensitive or Personally Identifiable Information (PII) into prompts, a company aims to sanitize the prompts before they are sent to the LLM provider.

elements.section-2411-011: Background on Differential Privacy for Text
elements.sent-2411-590: Also, the privacy guarantees of these methods hold for the entire text as they do not rely on the performance of a PII detector selecting entities to sanitize.
elements.sent-2411-591: See \cref{sec:sota for an overview of the state of the art in general text sanitization techniques.}
elements.sent-2411-592: For this work we leverage $\dx$-privacy, a relaxation of Local DP where the domain of input data is evaluated by an arbitrary distance metric $d$ \cite{chatzikokolakis2013Broadening .}
elements.sent-2411-593: In Local DP, the indistinguishability property applies equally to all elements of the input domain.
elements.sent-2411-594: This means that given a certain output, the mechanism must make it equally plausible that any input could have produced said output.
elements.sent-2411-595: Conversely, $\dx$-privacy supports indistinguishability relative to $d$, where similar inputs will produce more similar outputs, making said inputs distinguishable from other dissimilar inputs.
elements.sent-2411-596: Overall, $\dx$-privacy is designed to improve the utility over ordinary DP by acknowledging some information about the input in the output.
elements.sent-2411-597: An example of $\dx$-privacy is given in \cite{andres2013Geoindistinguishability , called Geo-indistinguishability, which avoids disclosure of a user's exact geographic location by releasing a differentially private substitute.}
elements.sent-2411-598: Sanitization of a text is then performed by adding differentially private noise to the embeddings of its tokens, for example by sampling noise via the Multidimensional Laplace Mechanism~\cite{feyisetan2020Privacy .}
elements.sent-2411-599: A randomized mechanism $M: \mathcal{X}\rightarrow\mathcal{Y}$ is said to be $\varepsilon \dx$-private~\cite{feyisetan2020Privacy if for any input $x_1, x_2 \in \mathcal{X}$ and any output $y\in \mathcal{Y}$: \begin{equation}\frac{Pr[M(x_1)=y]}{Pr[M(x_2)=y]}\leq e^{\varepsilon d(x_1, x_2)}\end{equation} Intuitively, consider the case of text-to-text sanitization where the output domain is also tokens and thus $\mathcal{Y =\mathcal{X}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-600: The closer two tokens $x_1$ and $x_2$ are in the embedding space according to the distance metric, the more similar the probabilities are that they will be mapped to the same output token $y$ by the mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-601: This means that tokens with similar meanings (i.e., close embeddings) are nearly indistinguishable from the perspective of the output distribution of the mechanism as they are almost equally likely to produce any given output token $y$.
elements.sent-2411-602: Note that Local DP is a special case of $\dx$-privacy where all distances are equal, i.e. $\forall x_1, x_2\in \mathcal{X : x_1\neq x_2 \Rightarrow d(x_1, x_2) = 1$.}
elements.sent-2411-603: The additional steps are outlined in lines 6 and 7: we first rank all the elements in $\mathcal{X}$ based on their distance to $e$ and then sample a replacement token from $\mathcal{X}$ where the probability of selecting each element is proportional to an exponential of its rank in the neighbor list of $e$ multiplied by $-\varepsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-604: As a result, $\varepsilon$ values in $\dx$-privacy are not directly comparable to those in ordinary DP, and their absolute values in this paper do not hold the same significance as the ones found in papers using DP on numerical data.
elements.sent-2411-605: Instead, an implementation rigorously following their definition (i.e., an exact nearest neighbor search) will output the input token 99\% of the time for most of our replicated experiments, not providing any privacy protection.
elements.sent-2411-606: In this section we motivate the need for more sophisticated means of assessing the privacy-utility tradeoff in prompt sanitization, followed by an overview of our proposed middleware architecture.
elements.subsection-2411-022: Utility and Privacy Trade-off for Prompts
elements.sent-2411-607: For clarity, in the rest of this paper we refer to this associated text as the prompt as we consider that the associated instruction does not require privacy protection.
elements.sent-2411-608: In particular, we consider the differentially-private text sanitization method described in \cref{subsec:santize (See \cref{sec:sota for an overview of other sanitization approaches).}}
elements.sent-2411-609: Because sanitization modifies the content of the prompt, it affects the performance of the LLM task performed on said prompt.
elements.sent-2411-610: This is referred to as the utility of the prompt and is typically measured by an appropriate evaluation metric (e.g., accuracy or semantic similarity) following standard usage in the privacy literature, e.g.~\cite{zhang2024No,feyisetan2020Privacy,qu2021Natural,yue2021Differential,wood2018Differential .}
elements.sent-2411-611: However, as mentioned in \cref{sec:backDX , $\varepsilon$ values are not similar to ordinary DP making it more challenging to select an appropriate value.}
elements.sent-2411-612: For example, the last text sample above is sanitized using $\varepsilon=35$, a value that would be considered non-private in ordinary DP, while it is still clearly too private to get any utility.
elements.sent-2411-613: We propose a solution architecture where a user benefits from a trusted middleware including an SLM to assess the utility of a given sanitization of a prompt.
elements.sent-2411-614: Intuitively, by analyzing the relationship between the original and sanitized prompts, as well as the SLM's performance on both, we believe the middleware is able to more accurately predict how the LLM will perform on the sanitized prompt.

elements.sent-2411-721: After PII has been detected, different solutions have been proposed to sanitize PII, either through simple methods of complete removal~\cite{papadopoulou2023Neural or placeholders~\cite{chen2023Hide .}}
elements.sent-2411-722: For more advanced methods, the PII can be sanitized via Differential Privacy (See \cref{sec:backDX ), Generalization or Rewriting (See below).}

elements.sent-2411-724: Both of these contributions apply a predetermined amount of differentially-private noise without regard for utility implications, and would benefit from a means to assess the utility of a given rewritten text, which we study in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-725: In our work, we use a text sanitization mechanism where the semantic is preserved through related tokens and the privacy-utility trade-off is navigable through our utility assessor architecture.

elements.subsection-2411-028: Extending to Other Mechanisms and Tasks
elements.sent-2411-729: In this paper, we experimented with a token-level sanitization mechanism based on DP presented in \cref{sec:backDX .}
elements.sent-2411-730: Yet, other sanitization mechanisms such as LLM-assisted Private Rewriting~\cite{igamberdiev2023DPBART will also trade utility for privacy and as such will benefit from our architecture to better navigate this tradeoff.}
elements.sent-2411-731: Our architecture can be used as long as the sanitization mechanism produces text as its output.
elements.sent-2411-732: Generalization to other language model tasks depends on the availability of a metric to measure the quality of a result without a reference (or ground-truth).
elements.sent-2411-733: Indeed, the Quality Estimator of \cref{fig:architecture must evaluate $p_\varepsilon$, $r^{SLM}$ and $r_\varepsilon^{SLM}$ without having such a reference.}
elements.sent-2411-734: As shown in \cref{sec:expe , cosine similarity with the original prompt is an example of such metric which can be applied on a summarization and a machine translation task.}
elements.sent-2411-735: Other language model tasks can be envisioned: Text Classification can be evaluated based on the confidence score of the SLM, or Open-ended Text Continuation with coherence and/or diversity.
elements.sent-2411-736: However, Named Entity Recognition and Question Answering are examples of tasks where, to the best of our knowledge, reference-free quality estimation appears difficult.
elements.sent-2411-737: In this paper, we assume that a prompt is processed only once through the middleware, and any two prompts submitted by the user are substantially different.
elements.sent-2411-738: In case of similar prompts, the middleware can reuse the previously saved sanitized version.
elements.sent-2411-739: This assumption is essential to ensure that the LLM provider does not see several sanitized versions of the same prompt, which could lead to an averaging attack similar to the one sketched in \cref{sec:sanit_perf_consideration .}
elements.sent-2411-740: Having said that, to assess the quality of the sanitized prompt, the middleware uses the original, sensitive prompt.
elements.sent-2411-741: Since this assessment is not differentially private, technically speaking, the decision to send the prompt to the LLM is not differentially private as a whole.
elements.sent-2411-742: To understand this, consider an extreme example where the user's prompt consists of only one token.
elements.sent-2411-743: The middleware may decide that the only ``sanitized'' version that satisfies the quality criterion is the original token itself.
elements.sent-2411-744: In this case, privacy is blatantly breached, as the LLM, knowing the decision criteria of the middleware, can guess that the received token is highly likely to be the original token.
elements.sent-2411-745: While this attack is possible in theory, in practice user prompts are expected to be sufficiently long, in which case it is difficult for the remote LLM to ascertain which tokens have been changed and which ones remain unaltered.
elements.sent-2411-746: Consider the case where quality is measured by cosine similarity and assume that the LLM knows the parameters of the middleware, including $\epsilon$ values and threshold for similarity.
elements.sent-2411-747: Upon receiving the sanitized prompt, the LLM only learns that the embedding vector of the sanitized prompt (i.e., the vector resulting from the average of the embeddings of its tokens) has a similarity with the embedding vector of the original prompt above the threshold.
elements.sent-2411-748: It does not learn the exact similarity value, and more importantly, the degree to which each token has participated in that similarity, given that each token is sanitized separately.
elements.sent-2411-749: Our goal is to conserve resources, both monetary for users and computational for the LLM provider, by predicting when sanitized prompts have lost their effectiveness for generating meaningful LLM responses.
elements.sent-2411-750: We show that it is difficult to anticipate the performance of an LLM on a sanitized prompt, and thus to prevent resource waste.
elements.sent-2411-751: We propose a middleware architecture including a locally trusted Small Language Model (SLM) to inform users about the utility/privacy trade-off before engaging with the LLM.
elements.sent-2411-752: We explore the potential of this architecture by conducting summarization and translation tasks with a publicly available dataset, two LLMs, and five SLMs of different capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-753: Our experiments are clear on the heavy and unintuitive impact sanitization can have on the utility of prompts.
elements.sent-2411-754: By leveraging numerical features available via our middleware, we train a regression model and confirm the benefit of our architecture where up to 20\% of the prompts previously wasting resources are now saved.
elements.sent-2411-755: In a company setting this is 20\% of all API calls saved, while precious computing resources are also saved on the LLM's provider side.
elements.sent-2411-756: During our study, we also shed light on an issue with $\dx$-privacy applied on text: an implementation strictly following the definition of one of its most-cited paper~\cite{feyisetan2020Privacy has a severely degraded privacy protection.}
elements.sent-2411-757: We acknowledge the limitations of our middleware, mainly its support for only those language model tasks whose quality can be evaluated without a reference, a criterion we argue already includes important tasks such as summarization and translation.
elements.sent-2411-758: Also, since prompts of poor quality are not sent in order to save resources, the LLM provider knows that a received prompt passed a certain quality threshold, which weakens the DP guarantees of the sanitization.
elements.sent-2411-759: We argue that in practice this information is of little use to the provider to break the privacy of the tokens.. \bibliographystyle{ACM-Reference-Format.}
